1: # Ruby AI eXtensions
2: 
3: ## What's Raix
4: 
5: Raix (pronounced "ray" because the x is silent) is a library that gives you everything you need to add discrete large-language model (LLM) AI components to your Ruby applications. Raix consists of proven code that has been extracted from [Olympia](https://olympia.chat), the world's leading virtual AI team platform, and probably one of the biggest and most successful AI chat projects written completely in Ruby.
6: 
7: Understanding how to use discrete AI components in otherwise normal code is key to productively leveraging Raix, and the subject of a book written by Raix's author Obie Fernandez, titled [Patterns of Application Development Using AI](https://leanpub.com/patterns-of-application-development-using-ai). You can easily support the ongoing development of this project by buying the book at Leanpub.
8: 
9: Raix 2.0 is powered by [RubyLLM](https://github.com/crmne/ruby_llm), giving you unified access to OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, and dozens of other providers through OpenRouter. Note that you can use Raix to add AI capabilities to non-Rails applications as long as you include ActiveSupport as a dependency.
10: 
11: ### Chat Completions
12: 
13: Raix consists of three modules that can be mixed in to Ruby classes to give them AI powers. The first (and mandatory) module is `ChatCompletion`, which provides `transcript` and `chat_completion` methods.
14: 
15: ```ruby
16: class MeaningOfLife
17:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
18: end
19: 
20: >> ai = MeaningOfLife.new
21: >> ai.transcript << { user: "What is the meaning of life?" }
22: >> ai.chat_completion
23: 
24: => "The question of the meaning of life is one of the most profound and enduring inquiries in philosophy, religion, and science.
25:     Different perspectives offer various answers..."
26: ```
27: 
28: By default, Raix will automatically add the AI's response to the transcript. This behavior can be controlled with the `save_response` parameter, which defaults to `true`. You may want to set it to `false` when making multiple chat completion calls during the lifecycle of a single object (whether sequentially or in parallel) and want to manage the transcript updates yourself:
29: 
30: ```ruby
31: >> ai.chat_completion(save_response: false)
32: ```
33: 
34: #### Transcript Format
35: 
36: The transcript accepts both abbreviated and standard OpenAI message hash formats. The abbreviated format, suitable for system, assistant, and user messages is simply a mapping of `role => content`, as shown in the example above.
37: 
38: ```ruby
39: transcript << { user: "What is the meaning of life?" }
40: ```
41: 
42: As mentioned, Raix also understands standard OpenAI messages hashes. The previous example could be written as:
43: 
44: ```ruby
45: transcript << { role: "user", content: "What is the meaning of life?" }
46: ```
47: 
48: One of the advantages of OpenRouter and the reason that it is used by default by this library is that it handles mapping message formats from the OpenAI standard to whatever other model you're wanting to use (Anthropic, Cohere, etc.)
49: 
50: Note that it's possible to override the current object's transcript by passing a `messages` array to `chat_completion`. This allows for multiple threads to share a single conversation context in parallel, by deferring when they write their responses back to the transcript.
51: 
52: ```
53: chat_completion(openai: "gpt-4.1-nano", messages: [{ user: "What is the meaning of life?" }])
54: ```
55: 
56: ### Predicted Outputs
57: 
58: Raix supports [Predicted Outputs](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/latency-optimization#use-predicted-outputs) with the `prediction` parameter for OpenAI.
59: 
60: ```ruby
61: >> ai.chat_completion(openai: "gpt-4o", params: { prediction: })
62: ```
63: 
64: ### Prompt Caching
65: 
66: Raix supports [Anthropic-style prompt caching](https://openrouter.ai/docs/prompt-caching#anthropic-claude) when using Anthropic's Claude family of models. You can specify a `cache_at` parameter when doing a chat completion. If the character count for the content of a particular message is longer than the cache_at parameter, it will be sent to Anthropic as a multipart message with a cache control "breakpoint" set to "ephemeral".
67: 
68: Note that there is a limit of four breakpoints, and the cache will expire within five minutes. Therefore, it is recommended to reserve the cache breakpoints for large bodies of text, such as character cards, CSV data, RAG data, book chapters, etc. Raix does not enforce a limit on the number of breakpoints, which means that you might get an error if you try to cache too many messages.
69: 
70: ```ruby
71: >> my_class.chat_completion(params: { cache_at: 1000 })
72: => {
73:   "messages": [
74:     {
75:       "role": "system",
76:       "content": [
77:         {
78:           "type": "text",
79:           "text": "HUGE TEXT BODY LONGER THAN 1000 CHARACTERS",
80:           "cache_control": {
81:             "type": "ephemeral"
82:           }
83:         }
84:       ]
85:     },
86: ```
87: 
88: ### JSON Mode
89: 
90: Raix supports JSON mode for chat completions, which ensures that the AI model's response is valid JSON. This is particularly useful when you need structured data from the model.
91: 
92: When using JSON mode with OpenAI models, Raix will automatically set the `response_format` parameter on requests accordingly, and attempt to parse the entire response body as JSON.
93: When using JSON mode with other models (e.g. Anthropic) that don't support `response_format`, Raix will look for JSON content inside of <json> XML tags in the response, before
94: falling back to parsing the entire response body. Make sure you tell the AI to reply with JSON inside of XML tags.
95: 
96: ```ruby
97: >> my_class.chat_completion(json: true)
98: => { "key": "value" }
99: ```
100: 
101: When using JSON mode with non-OpenAI providers, Raix automatically sets the `require_parameters` flag to ensure proper JSON formatting. You can also combine JSON mode with other parameters:
102: 
103: ```ruby
104: >> my_class.chat_completion(json: true, openai: "gpt-4o")
105: => { "key": "value" }
106: ```
107: 
108: ### before_completion Hook
109: 
110: The `before_completion` hook lets you intercept and modify chat completion requests before they're sent to the AI provider. This is useful for dynamic parameter resolution, logging, content filtering, PII redaction, and more.
111: 
112: #### Configuration Levels
113: 
114: Hooks can be configured at three levels, with later levels overriding earlier ones:
115: 
116: ```ruby
117: # Global level - applies to all chat completions
118: Raix.configure do |config|
119:   config.before_completion = ->(context) {
120:     # Return a hash of params to merge, or modify context.messages directly
121:     { temperature: 0.7 }
122:   }
123: end
124: 
125: # Class level - applies to all instances of a class
126: class MyAssistant
127:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
128: 
129:   configure do |config|
130:     config.before_completion = ->(context) { { model: "gpt-4o" } }
131:   end
132: end
133: 
134: # Instance level - applies to a single instance
135: assistant = MyAssistant.new
136: assistant.before_completion = ->(context) { { max_tokens: 500 } }
137: ```
138: 
139: When hooks exist at multiple levels, they're called in order (global → class → instance), with returned params merged together. Later hooks override earlier ones for the same parameter.
140: 
141: #### The CompletionContext Object
142: 
143: Hooks receive a `CompletionContext` object with access to:
144: 
145: ```ruby
146: context.chat_completion       # The ChatCompletion instance
147: context.messages              # Array of messages (mutable, in OpenAI format)
148: context.params                # Hash of params (mutable)
149: context.transcript            # The instance's transcript
150: context.current_model         # Currently configured model
151: context.chat_completion_class # The class including ChatCompletion
152: context.configuration         # The instance's configuration
153: ```
154: 
155: #### Use Cases
156: 
157: **Dynamic model selection from database:**
158: 
159: ```ruby
160: Raix.configure do |config|
161:   config.before_completion = ->(context) {
162:     settings = TenantSettings.find_by(tenant: Current.tenant)
163:     {
164:       model: settings.preferred_model,
165:       temperature: settings.temperature,
166:       max_tokens: settings.max_tokens
167:     }
168:   }
169: end
170: ```
171: 
172: **PII redaction:**
173: 
174: ```ruby
175: class SecureAssistant
176:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
177: 
178:   before_completion = ->(context) {
179:     context.messages.each do |msg|
180:       next unless msg[:content].is_a?(String)
181:       # Redact SSN patterns
182:       msg[:content] = msg[:content].gsub(/\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}/, "[SSN REDACTED]")
183:       # Redact email addresses
184:       msg[:content] = msg[:content].gsub(/[\w.-]+@[\w.-]+\.\w+/, "[EMAIL REDACTED]")
185:     end
186:     {} # Return empty hash if not modifying params
187:   }
188: end
189: ```
190: 
191: **Request logging:**
192: 
193: ```ruby
194: Raix.configure do |config|
195:   config.before_completion = ->(context) {
196:     Rails.logger.info({
197:       event: "chat_completion_request",
198:       model: context.current_model,
199:       message_count: context.messages.length,
200:       params: context.params.except(:messages)
201:     }.to_json)
202:     {} # Return empty hash, just logging
203:   }
204: end
205: ```
206: 
207: **Adding system prompts:**
208: 
209: ```ruby
210: assistant.before_completion = ->(context) {
211:   context.messages.unshift({
212:     role: "system",
213:     content: "Always be helpful and respectful."
214:   })
215:   {}
216: }
217: ```
218: 
219: **A/B testing models:**
220: 
221: ```ruby
222: Raix.configure do |config|
223:   config.before_completion = ->(context) {
224:     if Flipper.enabled?(:new_model, Current.user)
225:       { model: "gpt-4o" }
226:     else
227:       { model: "gpt-4o-mini" }
228:     end
229:   }
230: end
231: ```
232: 
233: Hooks can also be any object that responds to `#call`:
234: 
235: ```ruby
236: class CostTracker
237:   def call(context)
238:     # Track estimated cost based on message length
239:     estimated_tokens = context.messages.sum { |m| m[:content].to_s.length / 4 }
240:     StatsD.gauge("ai.estimated_input_tokens", estimated_tokens)
241:     {}
242:   end
243: end
244: 
245: Raix.configure do |config|
246:   config.before_completion = CostTracker.new
247: end
248: ```
249: 
250: ### Use of Tools/Functions
251: 
252: The second (optional) module that you can add to your Ruby classes after `ChatCompletion` is `FunctionDispatch`. It lets you declare and implement functions to be called at the AI's discretion in a declarative, Rails-like "DSL" fashion.
253: 
254: When the AI responds with tool function calls instead of a text message, Raix automatically:
255: 1. Executes the requested tool functions
256: 2. Adds the function results to the conversation transcript  
257: 3. Sends the updated transcript back to the AI for another completion
258: 4. Repeats this process until the AI responds with a regular text message
259: 
260: This automatic continuation ensures that tool calls are seamlessly integrated into the conversation flow. The AI can use tool results to formulate its final response to the user. You can limit the number of tool calls using the `max_tool_calls` parameter to prevent excessive function invocations.
261: 
262: ```ruby
263: class WhatIsTheWeather
264:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
265:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
266: 
267:   function :check_weather,
268:            "Check the weather for a location",
269:            location: { type: "string", required: true } do |arguments|
270:     "The weather in #{arguments[:location]} is hot and sunny"
271:   end
272: end
273: 
274: RSpec.describe WhatIsTheWeather do
275:   subject { described_class.new }
276: 
277:   it "provides a text response after automatically calling weather function" do
278:     subject.transcript << { user: "What is the weather in Zipolite, Oaxaca?" }
279:     response = subject.chat_completion(openai: "gpt-4o")
280:     expect(response).to include("hot and sunny")
281:   end
282: end
283: ```
284: 
285: Parameters are optional by default. Mark them as required with `required: true` or explicitly optional with `optional: true`.
286: 
287: Note that for security reasons, dispatching functions only works with functions implemented using `Raix::FunctionDispatch#function` or directly on the class.
288: 
289: #### Tool Filtering
290: 
291: You can control which tool functions are exposed to the AI per request using the `available_tools` parameter of the `chat_completion` method:
292: 
293: ```ruby
294: class WeatherAndTime
295:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
296:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
297: 
298:   function :check_weather, "Check the weather for a location", location: { type: "string" } do |arguments|
299:     "The weather in #{arguments[:location]} is sunny"
300:   end
301: 
302:   function :get_time, "Get the current time" do |_arguments|
303:     "The time is 12:00 PM"
304:   end
305: end
306: 
307: weather = WeatherAndTime.new
308: 
309: # Don't pass any tools to the LLM
310: weather.chat_completion(available_tools: false)
311: 
312: # Only pass specific tools to the LLM
313: weather.chat_completion(available_tools: [:check_weather])
314: 
315: # Pass all declared tools (default behavior)
316: weather.chat_completion
317: ```
318: 
319: The `available_tools` parameter accepts three types of values:
320: - `nil`: All declared tool functions are passed (default behavior)
321: - `false`: No tools are passed to the LLM
322: - An array of symbols: Only the specified tools are passed (raises `Raix::UndeclaredToolError` if a specified tool function is not declared)
323: 
324: #### Multiple Tool Calls
325: 
326: Some AI models (like GPT-4) can make multiple tool calls in a single response. When this happens, Raix will automatically handle all the function calls sequentially.
327: If you need to capture the arguments to the function calls, do so in the block passed to `function`. The response from `chat_completion` is always the final text
328: response from the assistant, and is not affected by function calls.
329: 
330: ```ruby
331: class MultipleToolExample
332:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
333:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
334: 
335:   attr_reader :invocations
336: 
337:   function :first_tool do |arguments|
338:     @invocations << :first
339:     "Result from first tool"
340:   end
341: 
342:   function :second_tool do |arguments|
343:     @invocations << :second
344:     "Result from second tool"
345:   end
346: 
347:   def initialize
348:     @invocations = []
349:   end
350: end
351: 
352: example = MultipleToolExample.new
353: example.transcript << { user: "Please use both tools" }
354: example.chat_completion(openai: "gpt-4o")
355: # => "I used both tools, as requested"
356: 
357: example.invocations
358: # => [:first, :second]
359: ```
360: 
361: #### Customizing Function Dispatch
362: 
363: You can customize how function calls are handled by overriding the `dispatch_tool_function` in your class. This is useful if you need to add logging, caching, error handling, or other custom behavior around function calls.
364: 
365: ```ruby
366: class CustomDispatchExample
367:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
368:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
369: 
370:   function :example_tool do |arguments|
371:     "Result from example tool"
372:   end
373: 
374:   def dispatch_tool_function(function_name, arguments)
375:     puts "Calling #{function_name} with #{arguments}"
376:     result = super
377:     puts "Result: #{result}"
378:     result
379:   end
380: end
381: ```
382: 
383: #### Function Call Caching
384: 
385: You can use ActiveSupport's Cache to cache function call results, which can be particularly useful for expensive operations or external API calls that don't need to be repeated frequently.
386: 
387: ```ruby
388: class CachedFunctionExample
389:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
390:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
391: 
392:   function :expensive_operation do |arguments|
393:     "Result of expensive operation with #{arguments}"
394:   end
395: 
396:   # Override dispatch_tool_function to enable caching for all functions
397:   def dispatch_tool_function(function_name, arguments)
398:     # Pass the cache to the superclass implementation
399:     super(function_name, arguments, cache: Rails.cache)
400:   end
401: end
402: ```
403: 
404: The caching mechanism works by:
405: 1. Passing the cache object through `dispatch_tool_function` to the function implementation
406: 2. Using the function name and arguments as cache keys
407: 3. Automatically fetching from cache when available or executing the function when not cached
408: 
409: This is particularly useful for:
410: - Expensive database operations
411: - External API calls
412: - Resource-intensive computations
413: - Functions with deterministic outputs for the same inputs
414: 
415: #### Limiting Tool Calls
416: 
417: You can control the maximum number of tool calls before the AI must provide a text response:
418: 
419: ```ruby
420: # Limit to 5 tool calls (default is 25)
421: response = my_ai.chat_completion(max_tool_calls: 5)
422: 
423: # Configure globally
424: Raix.configure do |config|
425:   config.max_tool_calls = 10
426: end
427: ```
428: 
429: #### Manually Stopping Tool Calls
430: 
431: For AI components that process tasks without end-user interaction, you can use `stop_tool_calls_and_respond!` within a function to force the AI to provide a text response without making additional tool calls.
432: 
433: ```ruby
434: class OrderProcessor
435:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
436:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
437: 
438:   SYSTEM_DIRECTIVE = "You are an order processor, tasked with order validation, inventory check,
439:                       payment processing, and shipping."
440: 
441:   attr_accessor :order
442: 
443:   def initialize(order)
444:     self.order = order
445:     transcript << { system: SYSTEM_DIRECTIVE }
446:     transcript << { user: order.to_json }
447:   end
448: 
449:   def perform
450:     # will automatically continue after tool calls until finished_processing is called
451:     chat_completion
452:   end
453: 
454: 
455:   # implementation of functions that can be called by the AI
456:   # entirely at its discretion, depending on the needs of the order.
457:   # The return value of each `perform` method will be added to the
458:   # transcript of the conversation as a function result.
459: 
460:   function :validate_order do
461:     OrderValidationWorker.perform(@order)
462:   end
463: 
464:   function :check_inventory do
465:     InventoryCheckWorker.perform(@order)
466:   end
467: 
468:   function :process_payment do
469:     PaymentProcessingWorker.perform(@order)
470:   end
471: 
472:   function :schedule_shipping do
473:     ShippingSchedulerWorker.perform(@order)
474:   end
475: 
476:   function :send_confirmation do
477:     OrderConfirmationWorker.perform(@order)
478:   end
479: 
480:   function :finished_processing do
481:     order.update!(transcript:, processed_at: Time.current)
482:     stop_tool_calls_and_respond!
483:     "Order processing completed successfully"
484:   end
485: end
486: ```
487: 
488: ### Prompt Declarations
489: 
490: The third (also optional) module that you can add mix in along with `ChatCompletion` is `PromptDeclarations`. It provides the ability to declare a "Prompt Chain" (series of prompts to be called in a sequence), and also features a declarative, Rails-like "DSL" of its own. Prompts can be defined inline or delegate to callable prompt objects, which themselves implement `ChatCompletion`.
491: 
492: The following example is a rough excerpt of the main "Conversation Loop" in Olympia, which pre-processes user messages to check for
493: the presence of URLs and scan memory before submitting as a prompt to GPT-4. Note that prompt declarations are executed in the order
494: that they are declared. The `FetchUrlCheck` callable prompt class is included for instructional purposes. Note that it is passed the
495: an instance of the object that is calling it in its initializer as its `context`. The passing of context means that you can assemble
496: composite prompt structures of arbitrary depth.
497: 
498: ```ruby
499: class PromptSubscriber
500:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
501:   include Raix::PromptDeclarations
502: 
503:   attr_accessor :conversation, :bot_message, :user_message
504: 
505:   # many other declarations omitted...
506: 
507:   prompt call: FetchUrlCheck
508: 
509:   prompt call: MemoryScan
510: 
511:   prompt text: -> { user_message.content }, stream: -> { ReplyStream.new(self) }, until: -> { bot_message.complete? }
512: 
513:   def initialize(conversation)
514:     self.conversation = conversation
515:   end
516: 
517:   def message_created(user_message)
518:     self.user_message = user_message
519:     self.bot_message = conversation.bot_message!(responding_to: user_message)
520: 
521:     chat_completion(loop: true, openai: "gpt-4o")
522:   end
523: 
524:   ...
525: end
526: 
527: class FetchUrlCheck
528:   include ChatCompletion
529:   include FunctionDispatch
530: 
531:   REGEX = %r{\b(?:http(s)?://)?(?:www\.)?[a-zA-Z0-9-]+(\.[a-zA-Z]{2,})+(/[^\s]*)?\b}
532: 
533:   attr_accessor :context, :conversation
534: 
535:   delegate :user_message, to: :context
536:   delegate :content, to: :user_message
537: 
538:   def initialize(context)
539:     self.context = context
540:     self.conversation = context.conversation
541:     self.model = "anthropic/claude-3-haiku"
542:   end
543: 
544:   def call
545:     return unless content&.match?(REGEX)
546: 
547:     transcript << { system: "Call the `fetch` function if the user mentions a website, otherwise say nil" }
548:     transcript << { user: content }
549: 
550:     chat_completion # TODO: consider looping to fetch more than one URL per user message
551:   end
552: 
553:   function :fetch, "Gets the plain text contents of a web page", url: { type: "string" } do |arguments|
554:     Tools::FetchUrl.fetch(arguments[:url]).tap do |result|
555:       parent = conversation.function_call!("fetch_url", arguments, parent: user_message)
556:       conversation.function_result!("fetch_url", result, parent:)
557:     end
558:   end
559: 
560: ```
561: 
562: Notably, Olympia does not use the `FunctionDispatch` module in its primary conversation loop because it does not have a fixed set of tools that are included in every single prompt. Functions are made available dynamically based on a number of factors including the user's plan tier and capabilities of the assistant with whom the user is conversing.
563: 
564: Streaming of the AI's response to the end user is handled by the `ReplyStream` class, passed to the final prompt declaration as its `stream` parameter. [Patterns of Application Development Using AI](https://leanpub.com/patterns-of-application-development-using-ai) devotes a whole chapter to describing how to write your own `ReplyStream` class.
565: 
566: #### Additional PromptDeclarations Options
567: 
568: The `PromptDeclarations` module supports several additional options that can be used to customize prompt behavior:
569: 
570: ```ruby
571: class CustomPromptExample
572:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
573:   include Raix::PromptDeclarations
574: 
575:   # Basic prompt with text
576:   prompt text: "Process this input"
577: 
578:   # Prompt with system directive
579:   prompt system: "You are a helpful assistant",
580:         text: "Analyze this text"
581: 
582:   # Prompt with conditions
583:   prompt text: "Process this input",
584:         if: -> { some_condition },
585:         unless: -> { some_other_condition }
586: 
587:   # Prompt with success callback
588:   prompt text: "Process this input",
589:         success: ->(response) { handle_response(response) }
590: 
591:   # Prompt with custom parameters
592:   prompt text: "Process with custom settings",
593:         params: { temperature: 0.7, max_tokens: 1000 }
594: 
595:   # Prompt with until condition for looping
596:   prompt text: "Keep processing until complete",
597:         until: -> { processing_complete? }
598: 
599:   # Prompt with raw response
600:   prompt text: "Get raw response",
601:         raw: true
602: 
603:   # Prompt using OpenAI directly
604:   prompt text: "Use OpenAI",
605:         openai: "gpt-4o"
606: end
607: ```
608: 
609: The available options include:
610: 
611: - `system`: Set a system directive for the prompt
612: - `if`/`unless`: Control prompt execution with conditions
613: - `success`: Handle prompt responses with callbacks
614: - `params`: Customize API parameters per prompt
615: - `until`: Control prompt looping
616: - `raw`: Get raw API responses
617: - `openai`: Use OpenAI directly
618: - `stream`: Control response streaming
619: - `call`: Delegate to callable prompt objects
620: 
621: You can also access the current prompt context and previous responses:
622: 
623: ```ruby
624: class ContextAwarePrompt
625:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
626:   include Raix::PromptDeclarations
627: 
628:   def process_with_context
629:     # Access current prompt
630:     current_prompt.params[:temperature]
631: 
632:     # Access previous response
633:     last_response
634: 
635:     chat_completion
636:   end
637: end
638: ```

640: ## Predicate Module
641: 
642: The `Raix::Predicate` module provides a simple way to handle yes/no/maybe questions using AI chat completion. It allows you to define blocks that handle different types of responses with their explanations. It is one of the concrete patterns described in the "Discrete Components" chapter of [Patterns of Application Development Using AI](https://leanpub.com/patterns-of-application-development-using-ai).
643: 
644: ### Usage
645: 
646: Include the `Raix::Predicate` module in your class and define handlers using block syntax:
647: 
648: ```ruby
649: class Question
650:   include Raix::Predicate
651: 
652:   yes? do |explanation|
653:     puts "Affirmative: #{explanation}"
654:   end
655: 
656:   no? do |explanation|
657:     puts "Negative: #{explanation}"
658:   end
659: 
660:   maybe? do |explanation|
661:     puts "Uncertain: #{explanation}"
662:   end
663: end
664: 
665: question = Question.new
666: question.ask("Is Ruby a programming language?")
667: # => Affirmative: Yes, Ruby is a dynamic, object-oriented programming language...
668: ```
669: 
670: ### Features
671: 
672: - Define handlers for yes, no, and/or maybe responses using the declarative class level block syntax.
673: - At least one handler (yes, no, or maybe) must be defined.
674: - Handlers receive the full AI response including explanation as an argument.
675: - Responses always start with "Yes, ", "No, ", or "Maybe, " followed by an explanation.
676: - Make sure to ask a question that can be answered with yes, no, or maybe (otherwise the results are indeterminate).
677: 
678: ### Example with Single Handler
679: 
680: You can define only the handlers you need:
681: 
682: ```ruby
683: class SimpleQuestion
684:   include Raix::Predicate
685: 
686:   # Only handle positive responses
687:   yes? do |explanation|
688:     puts "✅ #{explanation}"
689:   end
690: end
691: 
692: question = SimpleQuestion.new
693: question.ask("Is 2 + 2 = 4?")
694: # => ✅ Yes, 2 + 2 equals 4, this is a fundamental mathematical fact.
695: ```
696: 
697: ### Error Handling
698: 
699: The module will raise a RuntimeError if you attempt to ask a question without defining any response handlers:
700: 
701: ```ruby
702: class InvalidQuestion
703:   include Raix::Predicate
704: end
705: 
706: question = InvalidQuestion.new
707: question.ask("Any question")
708: # => RuntimeError: Please define a yes and/or no block
709: ```

711: ## Model Context Protocol (Experimental)
712: 
713: The `Raix::MCP` module provides integration with the Model Context Protocol, allowing you to connect your Raix-powered application to remote MCP servers. This feature is currently **experimental**.
714: 
715: ### Usage
716: 
717: Include the `Raix::MCP` module in your class and declare MCP servers using the `mcp` DSL:
718: 
719: ```ruby
720: class McpConsumer
721:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
722:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
723:   include Raix::MCP
724: 
725:   mcp "https://your-mcp-server.example.com/sse"
726: end
727: ```
728: 
729: ### Features
730: 
731: - Automatically fetches available tools from the remote MCP server using `tools/list`
732: - Registers remote tools as OpenAI-compatible function schemas
733: - Defines proxy methods that forward requests to the remote server via `tools/call`
734: - Seamlessly integrates with the existing `FunctionDispatch` workflow
735: - Handles transcript recording to maintain consistent conversation history
736: 
737: ### Filtering Tools
738: 
739: You can filter which remote tools to include:
740: 
741: ```ruby
742: class FilteredMcpConsumer
743:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
744:   include Raix::FunctionDispatch
745:   include Raix::MCP
746: 
747:   # Only include specific tools
748:   mcp "https://server.example.com/sse", only: [:tool_one, :tool_two]
749: 
750:   # Or exclude specific tools
751:   mcp "https://server.example.com/sse", except: [:tool_to_exclude]
752: end
753: ```
754: 
755: ## Response Format (Experimental)
756: 
757: The `ResponseFormat` class provides a way to declare a JSON schema for the response format of an AI chat completion. It's particularly useful when you need structured responses from AI models, ensuring the output conforms to your application's requirements.
758: 
759: ### Features
760: 
761: - Converts Ruby hashes and arrays into JSON schema format
762: - Supports nested structures and arrays
763: - Enforces strict validation with `additionalProperties: false`
764: - Automatically marks all top-level properties as required
765: - Handles both simple type definitions and complex nested schemas
766: 
767: ### Basic Usage
768: 
769: ```ruby
770: # Simple schema with basic types
771: format = Raix::ResponseFormat.new("PersonInfo", {
772:   name: { type: "string" },
773:   age: { type: "integer" }
774: })
775: 
776: # Use in chat completion
777: my_ai.chat_completion(response_format: format)
778: ```
779: 
780: ### Complex Structures
781: 
782: ```ruby
783: # Nested structure with arrays
784: format = Raix::ResponseFormat.new("CompanyInfo", {
785:   company: {
786:     name: { type: "string" },
787:     employees: [
788:       {
789:         name: { type: "string" },
790:         role: { type: "string" },
791:         skills: ["string"]
792:       }
793:     ],
794:     locations: ["string"]
795:   }
796: })
797: ```
798: 
799: ### Generated Schema
800: 
801: The ResponseFormat class generates a schema that follows this structure:
802: 
803: ```json
804: {
805:   "type": "json_schema",
806:   "json_schema": {
807:     "name": "SchemaName",
808:     "schema": {
809:       "type": "object",
810:       "properties": {
811:         "property1": { "type": "string" },
812:         "property2": { "type": "integer" }
813:       },
814:       "required": ["property1", "property2"],
815:       "additionalProperties": false
816:     },
817:     "strict": true
818:   }
819: }
820: ```
821: 
822: ### Using with Chat Completion
823: 
824: When used with chat completion, the AI model will format its response according to your schema:
825: 
826: ```ruby
827: class StructuredResponse
828:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
829: 
830:   def analyze_person(name)
831:     format = Raix::ResponseFormat.new("PersonAnalysis", {
832:       full_name: { type: "string" },
833:       age_estimate: { type: "integer" },
834:       personality_traits: ["string"]
835:     })
836: 
837:     transcript << { user: "Analyze the person named #{name}" }
838:     chat_completion(params: { response_format: format })
839:   end
840: end
841: 
842: response = StructuredResponse.new.analyze_person("Alice")
843: # Returns a hash matching the defined schema
844: ```

846: ## Installation
847: 
848: Install the gem and add to the application's Gemfile by executing:
849: 
850:     $ bundle add raix
851: 
852: If bundler is not being used to manage dependencies, install the gem by executing:
853: 
854:     $ gem install raix
855: 
856: ### Configuration
857: 
858: Raix 2.0 uses [RubyLLM](https://github.com/crmne/ruby_llm) as its backend for LLM provider connections. Configure your API keys through RubyLLM:
859: 
860: ```ruby
861: # config/initializers/raix.rb
862: RubyLLM.configure do |config|
863:   config.openrouter_api_key = ENV["OPENROUTER_API_KEY"]
864:   config.openai_api_key = ENV["OPENAI_API_KEY"]
865:   # Optional: configure other providers
866:   # config.anthropic_api_key = ENV["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]
867:   # config.gemini_api_key = ENV["GEMINI_API_KEY"]
868: end
869: ```
870: 
871: Raix will automatically use the appropriate provider based on the model name:
872: - Models starting with `gpt-` or `o1` use OpenAI directly
873: - All other models route through OpenRouter
874: 
875: ### Global vs Class-Level Configuration
876: 
877: You can configure Raix options globally or at the class level:
878: 
879: ```ruby
880: # Global configuration
881: Raix.configure do |config|
882:   config.temperature = 0.7
883:   config.max_tokens = 1000
884:   config.model = "gpt-4o"
885:   config.max_tool_calls = 25
886: end
887: 
888: # Class-level configuration (overrides global)
889: class MyAssistant
890:   include Raix::ChatCompletion
891: 
892:   configure do |config|
893:     config.model = "anthropic/claude-3-opus"
894:     config.temperature = 0.5
895:   end
896: end
897: ```
898: 
899: ### Upgrading from Raix 1.x
900: 
901: If upgrading from Raix 1.x, update your configuration from:
902: 
903: ```ruby
904: # Old 1.x configuration
905: Raix.configure do |config|
906:   config.openrouter_client = OpenRouter::Client.new(access_token: "...")
907:   config.openai_client = OpenAI::Client.new(access_token: "...")
908: end
909: ```
910: 
911: To the new RubyLLM-based configuration shown above.

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: require "active_support/concern"
4: require "active_support/core_ext/object/blank"
5: require "active_support/core_ext/string/filters"
6: require "active_support/core_ext/hash/indifferent_access"
7: require "active_support/core_ext/module/delegation"
8: require "ruby_llm"
9: 
10: module Raix
11:   class UndeclaredToolError < StandardError; end
12: 
13:   # The `ChatCompletion` module is a Rails concern that provides a way to interact
14:   # with the OpenRouter Chat Completion API via its client. The module includes a few
15:   # methods that allow you to build a transcript of messages and then send them to
16:   # the API for completion. The API will return a response that you can use however
17:   # you see fit.
18:   #
19:   # When the AI responds with tool function calls instead of a text message, this
20:   # module automatically:
21:   # 1. Executes the requested tool functions
22:   # 2. Adds the function results to the conversation transcript
23:   # 3. Sends the updated transcript back to the AI for another completion
24:   # 4. Repeats this process until the AI responds with a regular text message
25:   #
26:   # This automatic continuation ensures that tool calls are seamlessly integrated
27:   # into the conversation flow. The AI can use tool results to formulate its final
28:   # response to the user. You can limit the number of tool calls using the
29:   # `max_tool_calls` parameter to prevent excessive function invocations.
30:   #
31:   # Tool functions must be defined on the class that includes this module. The
32:   # `FunctionDispatch` module provides a Rails-like DSL for declaring these
33:   # functions at the class level, which is cleaner than implementing them as
34:   # instance methods.
35:   #
36:   # Note that some AI models can make multiple tool function calls in a single
37:   # response. When that happens, the module executes all requested functions
38:   # before continuing the conversation.
39:   module ChatCompletion
40:     extend ActiveSupport::Concern
41: 
42:     attr_accessor :before_completion, :cache_at, :frequency_penalty, :logit_bias, :logprobs, :loop, :min_p, :model,
43:                   :presence_penalty, :prediction, :repetition_penalty, :response_format, :stream, :temperature,
44:                   :max_completion_tokens, :max_tokens, :seed, :stop, :top_a, :top_k, :top_logprobs, :top_p, :tools,
45:                   :available_tools, :tool_choice, :provider, :max_tool_calls, :stop_tool_calls_and_respond
46: 
47:     class_methods do
48:       # Returns the current configuration of this class. Falls back to global configuration for unset values.
49:       def configuration
50:         @configuration ||= Configuration.new(fallback: Raix.configuration)
51:       end
52: 
53:       # Let's you configure the class-level configuration using a block.
54:       def configure
55:         yield(configuration)
56:       end
57:     end
58: 
59:     # Instance level access to the class-level configuration.
60:     delegate :configuration, to: :class
61: 
62:     # This method performs chat completion based on the provided transcript and parameters.
63:     #
64:     # @param params [Hash] The parameters for chat completion.
65:     # @option loop [Boolean] :loop (false) DEPRECATED - The system now automatically continues after tool calls.
66:     # @option params [Boolean] :json (false) Whether to return the parse the response as a JSON object. Will search for <json> tags in the response first, then fall back to the default JSON parsing of the entire response.
67:     # @option params [String] :openai (nil) If non-nil, use OpenAI with the model specified in this param.
68:     # @option params [Boolean] :raw (false) Whether to return the raw response or dig the text content.
69:     # @option params [Array] :messages (nil) An array of messages to use instead of the transcript.
70:     # @option tools [Array|false] :available_tools (nil) Tools to pass to the LLM. Ignored if nil (default). If false, no tools are passed. If an array, only declared tools in the array are passed.
71:     # @option max_tool_calls [Integer] :max_tool_calls Maximum number of tool calls before forcing a text response. Defaults to the configured value.
72:     # @return [String|Hash] The completed chat response.
73:     def chat_completion(params: {}, loop: false, json: false, raw: false, openai: nil, save_response: true, messages: nil, available_tools: nil, max_tool_calls: nil)
74:       # set params to default values if not provided
75:       params[:cache_at] ||= cache_at.presence
76:       params[:frequency_penalty] ||= frequency_penalty.presence
77:       params[:logit_bias] ||= logit_bias.presence
78:       params[:logprobs] ||= logprobs.presence
79:       params[:max_completion_tokens] ||= max_completion_tokens.presence || configuration.max_completion_tokens
80:       params[:max_tokens] ||= max_tokens.presence || configuration.max_tokens
81:       params[:min_p] ||= min_p.presence
82:       params[:prediction] = { type: "content", content: params[:prediction] || prediction } if params[:prediction] || prediction.present?
83:       params[:presence_penalty] ||= presence_penalty.presence
84:       params[:provider] ||= provider.presence
85:       params[:repetition_penalty] ||= repetition_penalty.presence
86:       params[:response_format] ||= response_format.presence
87:       params[:seed] ||= seed.presence
88:       params[:stop] ||= stop.presence
89:       params[:temperature] ||= temperature.presence || configuration.temperature
90:       params[:tool_choice] ||= tool_choice.presence
91:       params[:tools] = if available_tools == false
92:                          nil
93:                        elsif available_tools.is_a?(Array)
94:                          filtered_tools(available_tools)
95:                        else
96:                          tools.presence
97:                        end
98:       params[:top_a] ||= top_a.presence
99:       params[:top_k] ||= top_k.presence
100:       params[:top_logprobs] ||= top_logprobs.presence
101:       params[:top_p] ||= top_p.presence
102: 
103:       json = true if params[:response_format].is_a?(Raix::ResponseFormat)
104: 
105:       if json
106:         unless openai
107:           params[:provider] ||= {}
108:           params[:provider][:require_parameters] = true
109:         end
110:         if params[:response_format].blank?
111:           params[:response_format] ||= {}
112:           params[:response_format][:type] = "json_object"
113:         end
114:       end
115: 
116:       # Deprecation warning for loop parameter
117:       if loop
118:         warn "\n\nWARNING: The 'loop' parameter is DEPRECATED and will be ignored.\nChat completions now automatically continue after tool calls until the AI provides a text response.\nUse 'max_tool_calls' to limit the number of tool calls (default: #{configuration.max_tool_calls}).\n\n"
119:       end
120: 
121:       # Set max_tool_calls from parameter or configuration default
122:       self.max_tool_calls = max_tool_calls || configuration.max_tool_calls
123: 
124:       # Reset stop_tool_calls_and_respond flag
125:       @stop_tool_calls_and_respond = false
126: 
127:       # Track tool call count
128:       tool_call_count = 0
129: 
130:       # set the model to the default if not provided
131:       self.model ||= configuration.model
132: 
133:       adapter = MessageAdapters::Base.new(self)
134: 
135:       # duplicate the transcript to avoid race conditions in situations where
136:       # chat_completion is called multiple times in parallel
137:       # TODO: Defensive programming, ensure messages is an array
138:       messages ||= transcript.flatten.compact
139:       messages = messages.map { |msg| adapter.transform(msg) }.dup
140:       raise "Can't complete an empty transcript" if messages.blank?
141: 
142:       # Run before_completion hooks (global -> class -> instance)
143:       # Hooks can modify params and messages for logging, filtering, PII redaction, etc.
144:       run_before_completion_hooks(params, messages)
145: 
146:       begin
147:         response = ruby_llm_request(params:, model: openai || model, messages:, openai_override: openai)
148:         retry_count = 0
149:         content = nil
150: 
151:         # no need for additional processing if streaming
152:         return if stream && response.blank?
153: 
154:         # tuck the full response into a thread local in case needed
155:         Thread.current[:chat_completion_response] = response.is_a?(Hash) ? response.with_indifferent_access : response
156: 
157:         # TODO: add a standardized callback hook for usage events
158:         # broadcast(:usage_event, usage_subject, self.class.name.to_s, response, premium?)
159: 
160:         tool_calls = response.dig("choices", 0, "message", "tool_calls") || []
161:         if tool_calls.any?
162:           tool_call_count += tool_calls.size
163: 
164:           # Check if we've exceeded max_tool_calls
165:           if tool_call_count > self.max_tool_calls
166:             # Add system message about hitting the limit
167:             messages << { role: "system", content: "Maximum tool calls (#{self.max_tool_calls}) exceeded. Please provide a final response to the user without calling any more tools." }
168: 
169:             # Force a final response without tools
170:             params[:tools] = nil
171:             response = ruby_llm_request(params:, model: openai || model, messages:, openai_override: openai)
172: 
173:             # Process the final response
174:             content = response.dig("choices", 0, "message", "content")
175:             transcript << { assistant: content } if save_response
176:             return raw ? response : content.to_s.strip
177:           end
178: 
179:           # Dispatch tool calls
180:           tool_calls.each do |tool_call| # TODO: parallelize this?
181:             # dispatch the called function
182:             function_name = tool_call["function"]["name"]
183:             arguments = JSON.parse(tool_call["function"]["arguments"].presence || "{}")
184:             raise "Unauthorized function call: #{function_name}" unless self.class.functions.map { |f| f[:name].to_sym }.include?(function_name.to_sym)
185: 
186:             dispatch_tool_function(function_name, arguments.with_indifferent_access)
187:           end
188: 
189:           # After executing tool calls, we need to continue the conversation
190:           # to let the AI process the results and provide a text response.
191:           # We continue until the AI responds with a regular assistant message
192:           # (not another tool call request), unless stop_tool_calls_and_respond! was called.
193: 
194:           # Use the updated transcript for the next call, not the original messages
195:           updated_messages = transcript.flatten.compact
196:           last_message = updated_messages.last
197: 
198:           if !@stop_tool_calls_and_respond && (last_message[:role] != "assistant" || last_message[:tool_calls].present?)
199:             # Send the updated transcript back to the AI
200:             return chat_completion(
201:               params:,
202:               json:,
203:               raw:,
204:               openai:,
205:               save_response:,
206:               messages: nil, # Use transcript instead
207:               available_tools:,
208:               max_tool_calls: self.max_tool_calls - tool_call_count
209:             )
210:           elsif @stop_tool_calls_and_respond
211:             # If stop_tool_calls_and_respond was set, force a final response without tools
212:             params[:tools] = nil
213:             response = ruby_llm_request(params:, model: openai || model, messages:, openai_override: openai)
214: 
215:             content = response.dig("choices", 0, "message", "content")
216:             transcript << { assistant: content } if save_response
217:             return raw ? response : content.to_s.strip
218:           end
219:         end
220: 
221:         response.tap do |res|
222:           content = res.dig("choices", 0, "message", "content")
223: 
224:           transcript << { assistant: content } if save_response
225:           content = content.to_s.strip
226: 
227:           if json
228:             # Make automatic JSON parsing available to non-OpenAI providers that don't support the response_format parameter
229:             content = content.match(%r{<json>(.*?)</json>}m)[1] if content.include?("<json>")
230: 
231:             return JSON.parse(content)
232:           end
233: 
234:           return content unless raw
235:         end
236:       rescue JSON::ParserError => e
237:         if e.message.include?("not a valid") # blank JSON
238:           warn "Retrying blank JSON response... (#{retry_count} attempts) #{e.message}"
239:           retry_count += 1
240:           sleep 1 * retry_count # backoff
241:           retry if retry_count < 3
242: 
243:           raise e # just fail if we can't get content after 3 attempts
244:         end
245: 
246:         warn "Bad JSON received!!!!!!: #{content}"
247:         raise e
248:       rescue Faraday::BadRequestError => e
249:         # make sure we see the actual error message on console or Honeybadger
250:         warn "Chat completion failed!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!: #{e.response[:body]}"
251:         raise e
252:       end
253:     end
254: 
255:     # This method returns the transcript array.
256:     # Manually add your messages to it in the following abbreviated format
257:     # before calling `chat_completion`.
258:     #
259:     # { system: "You are a pumpkin" },
260:     # { user: "Hey what time is it?" },
261:     # { assistant: "Sorry, pumpkins do not wear watches" }
262:     #
263:     # to add a function call use the following format:
264:     # { function: { name: 'fancy_pants_function', arguments: { param: 'value' } } }
265:     #
266:     # to add a function result use the following format:
267:     # { function: result, name: 'fancy_pants_function' }
268:     #
269:     # @return [Array] The transcript array.
270:     def transcript
271:       @transcript ||= TranscriptAdapter.new(ruby_llm_chat)
272:     end
273: 
274:     # Returns the RubyLLM::Chat instance for this conversation
275:     def ruby_llm_chat
276:       @ruby_llm_chat ||= begin
277:         model_id = model || configuration.model
278: 
279:         # Determine provider based on model format or explicit openai flag
280:         provider = if model_id.to_s.start_with?("openai/") || model_id.to_s.match?(/^gpt-/)
281:                      :openai
282:                    else
283:                      :openrouter
284:                    end
285: 
286:         RubyLLM.chat(model: model_id, provider:, assume_model_exists: true)
287:       end
288:     end
289: 
290:     # Dispatches a tool function call with the given function name and arguments.
291:     # This method can be overridden in subclasses to customize how function calls are handled.
292:     #
293:     # @param function_name [String] The name of the function to call
294:     # @param arguments [Hash] The arguments to pass to the function
295:     # @param cache [ActiveSupport::Cache] Optional cache object
296:     # @return [Object] The result of the function call
297:     def dispatch_tool_function(function_name, arguments, cache: nil)
298:       public_send(function_name, arguments, cache)
299:     end
300: 
301:     private
302: 
303:     def filtered_tools(tool_names)
304:       return nil if tool_names.blank?
305: 
306:       requested_tools = tool_names.map(&:to_sym)
307:       available_tool_names = tools.map { |tool| tool.dig(:function, :name).to_sym }
308: 
309:       undeclared_tools = requested_tools - available_tool_names
310:       raise UndeclaredToolError, "Undeclared tools: #{undeclared_tools.join(", ")}" if undeclared_tools.any?
311: 
312:       tools.select { |tool| requested_tools.include?(tool.dig(:function, :name).to_sym) }
313:     end
314: 
315:     def run_before_completion_hooks(params, messages)
316:       hooks = [
317:         Raix.configuration.before_completion,
318:         self.class.configuration.before_completion,
319:         before_completion
320:       ].compact
321: 
322:       return if hooks.empty?
323: 
324:       context = CompletionContext.new(
325:         chat_completion: self,
326:         messages:,
327:         params:
328:       )
329: 
330:       hooks.each do |hook|
331:         result = hook.call(context) if hook.respond_to?(:call)
332:         next unless result.is_a?(Hash)
333: 
334:         # Handle model separately since it's passed as a keyword arg to ruby_llm_request
335:         self.model = result[:model] if result.key?(:model)
336:         params.merge!(result.compact)
337:       end
338:     end
339: 
340:     def ruby_llm_request(params:, model:, messages:, openai_override: nil)
341:       # Create a temporary chat instance for this request
342:       provider = determine_provider(model, openai_override)
343:       chat = RubyLLM.chat(model:, provider:, assume_model_exists: true)
344: 
345:       # Apply messages to the chat
346:       # Track if we have a user message to determine how to call ask
347:       has_user_message = false
348: 
349:       messages.each do |msg|
350:         role = msg[:role] || msg["role"]
351:         content = msg[:content] || msg["content"]
352: 
353:         case role.to_s
354:         when "system"
355:           chat.with_instructions(content)
356:         when "user"
357:           has_user_message = true
358:           chat.add_message(role: :user, content: MultimodalContentAdapter.translate(content))
359:         when "assistant"
360:           if msg[:tool_calls] || msg["tool_calls"]
361:             chat.add_message(role: :assistant, content:, tool_calls: msg[:tool_calls] || msg["tool_calls"])
362:           else
363:             chat.add_message(role: :assistant, content:)
364:           end
365:         when "tool"
366:           chat.add_message(
367:             role: :tool,
368:             content:,
369:             tool_call_id: msg[:tool_call_id] || msg["tool_call_id"]
370:           )
371:         end
372:       end
373: 
374:       # Apply configuration parameters
375:       chat.with_temperature(params[:temperature]) if params[:temperature]
376: 
377:       # Apply additional params (RubyLLM with_params expects keyword args)
378:       additional_params = params.compact.except(:temperature, :tools, :max_tokens, :max_completion_tokens)
379:       chat.with_params(**additional_params) if additional_params.any?
380: 
381:       # Handle tools - convert Raix function declarations to RubyLLM tools
382:       if params[:tools].present? && respond_to?(:class) && self.class.respond_to?(:functions)
383:         ruby_llm_tools = FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm(self)
384:         ruby_llm_tools.each { |tool| chat.with_tool(tool) }
385:       end
386: 
387:       # Execute the completion
388:       if stream.present?
389:         # Streaming mode
390:         if has_user_message
391:           chat.complete(&stream)
392:         else
393:           chat.ask(&stream)
394:         end
395:         nil # Return nil for streaming as per original behavior
396:       else
397:         # Non-streaming mode - return OpenAI-compatible response format
398:         response_message = has_user_message ? chat.complete : chat.ask
399: 
400:         # Pull through the raw provider payload when available. OpenRouter's
401:         # `id` is the only handle we have to look up authoritative billing
402:         # cost via /api/v1/generation, and callers that watch the response
403:         # snapshot for `model` / cached-token counts shouldn't have to break
404:         # out of the OpenAI-compatible shape to get them.
405:         raw_body = response_message.raw.respond_to?(:body) ? response_message.raw.body : nil
406:         raw_body = {} unless raw_body.is_a?(Hash)
407: 
408:         usage_payload = {
409:           "prompt_tokens" => response_message.input_tokens,
410:           "completion_tokens" => response_message.output_tokens,
411:           "total_tokens" => (response_message.input_tokens || 0) + (response_message.output_tokens || 0)
412:         }
413: 
414:         # Merge prompt_tokens_details / completion_tokens_details (cached tokens,
415:         # reasoning tokens) when the provider supplied them.
416:         if (upstream_usage = raw_body["usage"]).is_a?(Hash)
417:           upstream_usage.each do |key, value|
418:             next if usage_payload.key?(key)
419: 
420:             usage_payload[key] = value
421:           end
422:         end
423: 
424:         {
425:           "id" => raw_body["id"],
426:           "model" => raw_body["model"] || response_message.model_id,
427:           "provider" => raw_body["provider"],
428:           "choices" => [
429:             {
430:               "message" => {
431:                 "role" => "assistant",
432:                 "content" => response_message.content,
433:                 "tool_calls" => response_message.tool_calls
434:               },
435:               "finish_reason" => response_message.tool_call? ? "tool_calls" : "stop"
436:             }
437:           ],
438:           "usage" => usage_payload
439:         }
440:       end
441:     rescue StandardError => e
442:       warn "RubyLLM request failed: #{e.message}"
443:       raise e
444:     end
445: 
446:     def determine_provider(model, openai_override)
447:       return :openai if openai_override
448:       return :openai if model.to_s.match?(/^gpt-/) || model.to_s.match?(/^o\d/)
449: 
450:       # Default to openrouter for model IDs with provider prefix
451:       :openrouter
452:     end
453:   end
454: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: require "securerandom"
4: module Raix
5:   # Provides declarative function definition for ChatCompletion classes.
6:   #
7:   # Example:
8:   #
9:   #   class MeaningOfLife
10:   #     include Raix::ChatCompletion
11:   #     include Raix::FunctionDispatch
12:   #
13:   #     function :ask_deep_thought do
14:   #       wait 236_682_000_000_000
15:   #       "The meaning of life is 42"
16:   #     end
17:   #
18:   #     def initialize
19:   #       transcript << { user: "What is the meaning of life?" }
20:   #       chat_completion
21:   #     end
22:   #   end
23:   module FunctionDispatch
24:     extend ActiveSupport::Concern
25: 
26:     class_methods do
27:       attr_reader :functions
28: 
29:       # Defines a function that can be dispatched by the ChatCompletion module while
30:       # processing the response from an AI model.
31:       #
32:       # Declaring a function here will automatically add it (in JSON Schema format) to
33:       # the list of tools provided to the OpenRouter Chat Completion API. The function
34:       # will be dispatched by name, so make sure the name is unique. The function's block
35:       # argument will be executed in the instance context of the class that includes this module.
36:       #
37:       # Example:
38:       #   function :google_search, "Search Google for something", query: { type: "string" } do |arguments|
39:       #     GoogleSearch.new(arguments[:query]).search
40:       #   end
41:       #
42:       # @param name [Symbol] The name of the function.
43:       # @param description [String] An optional description of the function.
44:       # @param parameters [Hash] The parameters that the function accepts.
45:       # @param block [Proc] The block of code to execute when the function is called.
46:       def function(name, description = nil, **parameters, &block)
47:         @functions ||= []
48:         @functions << begin
49:           {
50:             name:,
51:             parameters: { type: "object", properties: {}, required: [] }
52:           }.tap do |definition|
53:             definition[:description] = description if description.present?
54:             parameters.each do |key, value|
55:               value = value.dup
56:               required = value.delete(:required)
57:               optional = value.delete(:optional)
58:               definition[:parameters][:properties][key] = value
59:               if required || optional == false
60:                 definition[:parameters][:required] << key
61:               end
62:             end
63:             definition[:parameters].delete(:required) if definition[:parameters][:required].empty?
64:           end
65:         end
66: 
67:         define_method(name) do |arguments, cache|
68:           id = SecureRandom.uuid[0, 23]
69: 
70:           content = if cache.present?
71:                       cache.fetch([name, arguments]) do
72:                         instance_exec(arguments, &block)
73:                       end
74:                     else
75:                       instance_exec(arguments, &block)
76:                     end
77: 
78:           # add in one operation to prevent race condition and potential wrong
79:           # interleaving of tool calls in multi-threaded environments
80:           transcript << [
81:             {
82:               role: "assistant",
83:               content: nil,
84:               tool_calls: [
85:                 {
86:                   id:,
87:                   type: "function",
88:                   function: {
89:                     name:,
90:                     arguments: arguments.to_json
91:                   }
92:                 }
93:               ]
94:             },
95:             {
96:               role: "tool",
97:               tool_call_id: id,
98:               name:,
99:               content: content.to_s
100:             }
101:           ]
102: 
103:           # Return the content - ChatCompletion will automatically continue
104:           # the conversation after tool execution to get a final response
105:           content
106:         end
107:       end
108:     end
109: 
110:     included do
111:       attr_accessor :chat_completion_args
112:     end
113: 
114:     def chat_completion(**chat_completion_args)
115:       self.chat_completion_args = chat_completion_args
116:       super
117:     end
118: 
119:     # Stops the automatic continuation of chat completions after this function call.
120:     # Useful when you want to halt processing within a function and force the AI
121:     # to provide a text response without making additional tool calls.
122:     def stop_tool_calls_and_respond!
123:       @stop_tool_calls_and_respond = true
124:     end
125: 
126:     def tools
127:       return [] unless self.class.functions
128: 
129:       self.class.functions.map { |function| { type: "function", function: } }
130:     end
131:   end
132: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: module Raix
4:   # Adapter to convert Raix function declarations to RubyLLM::Tool instances
5:   class FunctionToolAdapter
6:     def self.create_tool_from_function(function_def, instance)
7:       tool_class = Class.new(RubyLLM::Tool) do
8:         description function_def[:description] if function_def[:description]
9: 
10:         # Forward the full JSON-schema parameter dict to RubyLLM rather than
11:         # rebuilding it field-by-field via `param(...)`. The per-field path
12:         # only carried `type` and `description`, which silently dropped richer
13:         # schema like `additionalProperties`, `items`, `enum`, or nested
14:         # `properties` — leaving providers (notably Gemini's structured output)
15:         # to invent degenerate shapes for `type: object` arguments.
16:         if function_def[:parameters].is_a?(Hash) && function_def[:parameters][:properties].present?
17:           # RubyLLM's `params(schema)` path forwards the schema verbatim and, unlike the
18:           # per-field `param(...)` path, does not inject the OpenAI strict-mode guards. Default
19:           # them on so existing tools keep strict behavior, while letting a function declaration
20:           # override either by setting it explicitly.
21:           params({ additionalProperties: false, strict: true }.merge(function_def[:parameters]))
22:         end
23: 
24:         # Store reference to the instance and function name
25:         define_method(:raix_instance) { instance }
26:         define_method(:raix_function_name) { function_def[:name] }
27: 
28:         # Override execute to call the Raix function
29:         define_method(:execute) do |**args|
30:           raix_instance.public_send(raix_function_name, args.with_indifferent_access, nil)
31:         end
32:       end
33: 
34:       # Set a meaningful name for the tool class
35:       tool_class.define_singleton_method(:name) do
36:         "Raix::GeneratedTool::#{function_def[:name].to_s.camelize}"
37:       end
38: 
39:       tool_instance = tool_class.new
40: 
41:       # Override the name method to return the original function name
42:       # This ensures RubyLLM can match the tool call from the AI
43:       tool_instance.define_singleton_method(:name) do
44:         function_def[:name].to_s
45:       end
46: 
47:       tool_instance
48:     end
49: 
50:     def self.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm(raix_instance)
51:       return [] unless raix_instance.class.respond_to?(:functions)
52:       return [] if raix_instance.class.functions.blank?
53: 
54:       raix_instance.class.functions.map do |function_def|
55:         create_tool_from_function(function_def, raix_instance)
56:       end
57:     end
58:   end
59: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: require "active_support/core_ext/hash/indifferent_access"
4: require "base64"
5: require "stringio"
6: 
7: module Raix
8:   # Translates OpenAI-style multimodal content arrays (a `text` part plus one or
9:   # more `image_url` parts) into a RubyLLM::Content so images survive the trip to
10:   # the provider.
11:   #
12:   # RubyLLM's `add_message`/`ask` treat a raw array of OpenAI content hashes as
13:   # plain text, so an `{ type: "image_url", image_url: { url: ... } }` part is
14:   # silently dropped and a vision model receives text only. See
15:   # https://github.com/OlympiaAI/raix/issues/51
16:   #
17:   # Anything that is not an array of hashes containing at least one `image_url`
18:   # part is returned untouched, so existing text completions are unaffected.
19:   class MultimodalContentAdapter
20:     def self.translate(content)
21:       new(content).translate
22:     end
23: 
24:     def initialize(content)
25:       @content = content
26:     end
27: 
28:     def translate
29:       return @content unless translatable?
30: 
31:       parts = @content.map(&:with_indifferent_access)
32:       attachments = parts.select { |part| part[:type].to_s == "image_url" }
33:                          .filter_map { |part| attachment_source(part.dig(:image_url, :url)) }
34:       return @content if attachments.empty?
35: 
36:       text = parts.select { |part| part[:type].to_s == "text" }.filter_map { |part| part[:text] }.join("\n")
37:       RubyLLM::Content.new(text.empty? ? nil : text, attachments)
38:     end
39: 
40:     private
41: 
42:     def translatable?
43:       @content.is_a?(Array) &&
44:         @content.all? { |part| part.is_a?(Hash) } &&
45:         @content.any? { |part| (part[:type] || part["type"]).to_s == "image_url" }
46:     end
47: 
48:     # RubyLLM::Attachment recognizes http(s) URLs, file paths, and IO objects, but
49:     # not base64 `data:` URIs (it would treat one as a filesystem path). Decode
50:     # those into a binary StringIO, which Attachment handles as an IO source.
51:     def attachment_source(url)
52:       return if url.nil? || url.empty?
53:       return url unless url.start_with?("data:")
54: 
55:       match = url.match(/\Adata:[^;,]*;base64,(.+)\z/m)
56:       return url unless match
57: 
58:       io = StringIO.new(Base64.decode64(match[1]))
59:       io.set_encoding(Encoding::BINARY) if io.respond_to?(:set_encoding)
60:       io
61:     end
62:   end
63: end

1: # frozen_string_literal: true
2: 
3: module Raix
4:   # Adapter to convert between Raix's transcript array format and RubyLLM's Message objects
5:   class TranscriptAdapter
6:     attr_reader :ruby_llm_chat
7: 
8:     def initialize(ruby_llm_chat)
9:       @ruby_llm_chat = ruby_llm_chat
10:       @pending_messages = []
11:     end
12: 
13:     # Add a message in Raix format (hash) to the transcript
14:     def <<(message_hash)
15:       case message_hash
16:       when Array
17:         # Handle nested arrays (from function dispatch)
18:         message_hash.each { |msg| self << msg }
19:       when Hash
20:         add_message_from_hash(message_hash)
21:       end
22:       self
23:     end
24: 
25:     # Return all messages in Raix-compatible format
26:     def flatten
27:       ruby_llm_messages = @ruby_llm_chat.messages.map { |msg| message_to_raix_format(msg) }
28:       pending = @pending_messages.map { |msg| normalize_message_format(msg) }
29:       (ruby_llm_messages + pending).flatten
30:     end
31: 
32:     # Get all messages including pending ones
33:     def to_a
34:       flatten
35:     end
36: 
37:     # Allow iteration
38:     def compact
39:       flatten.compact
40:     end
41: 
42:     # Clear all messages
43:     def clear
44:       @ruby_llm_chat.reset_messages!
45:       @pending_messages.clear
46:       self
47:     end
48: 
49:     # Get last message
50:     def last
51:       flatten.last
52:     end
53: 
54:     # Get size of transcript
55:     def size
56:       flatten.size
57:     end
58: 
59:     alias length size
60: 
61:     private
62: 
63:     def add_message_from_hash(hash)
64:       # Raix abbreviated format: { system: "text" }, { user: "text" }, { assistant: "text" }
65:       if hash.key?(:system) || hash.key?("system")
66:         content = hash[:system] || hash["system"]
67:         @ruby_llm_chat.with_instructions(content)
68:         @pending_messages << { role: "system", content: }
69:       elsif hash.key?(:user) || hash.key?("user")
70:         content = hash[:user] || hash["user"]
71:         # Don't add to ruby_llm_chat yet - wait for chat_completion call
72:         @pending_messages << { role: "user", content: }
73:       elsif hash.key?(:assistant) || hash.key?("assistant")
74:         content = hash[:assistant] || hash["assistant"]
75:         @pending_messages << { role: "assistant", content: }
76:       elsif hash[:role] || hash["role"]
77:         # Standard OpenAI format (tool messages, assistant with tool_calls, etc.)
78:         @pending_messages << hash.with_indifferent_access
79:       end
80:     end
81: 
82:     def message_to_raix_format(message)
83:       # Return in Raix abbreviated format { system: "...", user: "...", assistant: "..." }
84:       # unless it's a tool message which needs full format
85:       if message.tool_call? || message.tool_result?
86:         result = {
87:           role: message.role.to_s,
88:           content: message.content
89:         }
90:         result[:tool_calls] = message.tool_calls if message.tool_call?
91:         result[:tool_call_id] = message.tool_call_id if message.tool_result?
92:         result
93:       else
94:         # Use abbreviated format
95:         { message.role.to_sym => message.content }
96:       end
97:     end
98: 
99:     def normalize_message_format(msg)
100:       # If already in abbreviated format, return as-is
101:       return msg if msg.key?(:system) || msg.key?(:user) || msg.key?(:assistant)
102:       return msg if msg["system"] || msg["user"] || msg["assistant"]
103: 
104:       # If in standard format with role/content, convert to abbreviated
105:       if msg[:role] || msg["role"]
106:         role = (msg[:role] || msg["role"]).to_sym
107:         content = msg[:content] || msg["content"]
108: 
109:         # Tool messages stay in full format
110:         if msg[:tool_calls] || msg["tool_calls"] || msg[:tool_call_id] || msg["tool_call_id"]
111:           return msg
112:         end
113: 
114:         # Convert to abbreviated format
115:         { role => content }
116:       else
117:         msg
118:       end
119:     end
120:   end
